The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Access the books data
books_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:2']

# Load books data (it's stored in a file)
if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_storage

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Extract publication years from details field
year_book_mapping = []
no_year_count = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    if not details:
        no_year_count += 1
        continue
    
    # Look for patterns like "released on January 1, 2004" or "published on May 20, 1996"
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Check if it's a reasonable publication year
        if 1900 <= year <= 2030:
            year_book_mapping.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'title': book['title'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': f"{year//10*10}s"
            })
        else:
            no_year_count += 1
    else:
        no_year_count += 1

print(f'Successfully extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books')
print(f'Failed to extract year for {no_year_count} books')

if year_book_mapping:
    years = [item['year'] for item in year_book_mapping]
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample data: {year_book_mapping[:3]}')

# Now load the review data to get ratings
review_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:9']
if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    review_data = review_storage

print(f'\nLoaded {len(review_data)} reviews')
print(f'Sample review: {review_data[0]}')

# Create mapping of purchase_id to rating
purchase_rating_map = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    if purchase_id:
        if purchase_id not in purchase_rating_map:
            purchase_rating_map[purchase_id] = []
        purchase_rating_map[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f'Found {len(purchase_rating_map)} unique books with reviews')

# Match books with their reviews and calculate average rating per decade
from collections import defaultdict

decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'books': set()})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Look for purchase_id that matches book_id pattern
    # The hint says purchase_id and book_id refer to same entities but aren't exact matches
    # Let's try to match by extracting the numeric part
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        book_num = match.group(1)
        # Look for purchase_id with same number
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_rating_map.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate average
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'total_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade statistics (with at least 10 books):')
for result in decade_results:
    print(f"{result['decade']}: {result['avg_rating']} avg rating, {result['book_count']} books, {result['total_reviews']} reviews")

# Return the top decade
if decade_results:
    top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print(f'\nTop decade: {top_decade}')
else:
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')
    top_decade = None

# Print results in JSON format
output = {
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'decade_rankings': decade_results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

# Now load the review data to get ratings

else:

print(f'\nLoaded {len(review_data)} reviews')

# Create mapping of purchase_id to rating

for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating', '0')
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        rating = 0.0
    
    if purchase_id:
        if purchase_id not in purchase_rating_map:
            purchase_rating_map[purchase_id] = []
        purchase_rating_map[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Match books with their reviews and calculate average rating per decade

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Extract numeric part from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" -> "1")
    book_id_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if book_id_match:
        book_num = book_id_match.group(1)
        # Look for purchase_id containing the same number
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_rating_map.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
                break  # Found match, move to next book

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate average
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'total_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade statistics (with at least 10 books):')
for result in decade_results:
    print(str(result['decade']) + ': ' + str(result['avg_rating']) + ' avg rating, ' + 
          str(result['book_count']) + ' books, ' + str(result['total_reviews']) + ' reviews')

# Return the top decade
if decade_results:
    top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print('\nTop decade: ' + top_decade)
else:
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')
    top_decade = None

# Print results in JSON format
output = {
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'decade_rankings': decade_results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Storage keys should be available
print("Available storage keys:")
for key in list(locals().keys()):
    if 'var_functions' in key:
        print(f"  {key}: {type(locals()[key])}")

# Load books data from storage
books_key = 'var_functions.query_db:24'
if books_key not in locals():
    print("Books data not found in storage")
    books_data = []
else:
    books_storage = locals()[books_key]
    if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
        with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
            books_data = json.load(f)
    else:
        books_data = books_storage
    print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books")

# Load review data from storage  
review_key = 'var_functions.query_db:9'
if review_key not in locals():
    print("Review data not found in storage")
    review_data = []
else:
    review_storage = locals()[review_key]
    if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
        with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
            review_data = json.load(f)
    else:
        review_data = review_storage
    print(f"Loaded {len(review_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication years from books
year_book_mapping = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit year between 1900-2030
    match = re.search(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-3]\d)', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        year_book_mapping.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books")

# Create purchase_id -> ratings mapping
purchase_ratings = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    if purchase_id:
        try:
            rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
            if purchase_id not in purchase_ratings:
                purchase_ratings[purchase_id] = []
            purchase_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)
        except:
            continue

print(f"Mapped ratings for {len(purchase_ratings)} purchase IDs")

# Calculate decade statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'books': set(), 'review_count': 0})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Extract numeric part from book_id
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        book_num = match.group(1)
        # Find matching purchase_id
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_ratings.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:  # Fuzzy match by number
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                break

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count
        })

# Sort by rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"\nFound {len(decade_results)} decades with >=10 books")
print("Rankings:")
for r in decade_results:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}: {r['avg_rating']} ({r['book_count']} books)")

# Get top decade
top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else None

output = {'top_decade': top_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load books data
books_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
books_data = []
if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_storage

# Load review data
review_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:9']
review_data = []
if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    review_data = review_storage

print('Books:', len(books_data))
print('Reviews:', len(review_data))

# Extract years and create book-year mapping
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    m = re.search(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-3]\d)', details)
    if m:
        year = int(m.group(1))
        book_years[book['book_id']] = year

print('Books with year:', len(book_years))

# Map purchase_id to ratings
purchase_ratings = {}
for review in review_data:
    pid = review.get('purchase_id')
    if pid:
        try:
            rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
            purchase_ratings.setdefault(pid, []).append(rating)
        except:
            pass

print('Purchase IDs:', len(purchase_ratings))

# Calculate decade stats
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'total': 0, 'books': set()})

for book_id, year in book_years.items():
    book_num = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if not book_num: continue
    book_num = book_num.group(1)
    
    for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_ratings.items():
        if book_num in purchase_id:
            avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
            decade = str((year // 10) * 10) + 's'
            decades[decade]['total'] += avg
            decades[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
            break

# Filter and rank
results = []
for decade, data in decades.items():
    book_count = len(data['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = data['total'] / book_count
        results.append([decade, round(avg_rating, 2), book_count])

results.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print('Decades with >=10 books:', len(results))
for r in results:
    print(r[0], r[1], r[2])

top = results[0][0] if results else None
print('Top decade:', top)

out = {'top_decade': top, 'all': results}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(out))"}

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:9 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books_info data
with open(var_functions.query_db:2, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Check the details field to understand the structure
print('Sample details fields:')
for i, book in enumerate(books_data[:5]):
    print(f"Book {i+1}: {book['details'][:200]}")
    
# Try to extract year from details
years = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Look for 4-digit years
    year_matches = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_matches:
        # Usually the publication year is between 1900-2030
        for year in year_matches:
            year_int = int(year)
            if 1900 <= year_int <= 2030:
                years.append(year_int)
                break

print(f'\nExtracted {len(years)} years from {len(books_data)} books')
if years:
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample years: {years[:10]}')"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books_info data from the storage variable
books_raw = locals()['var_functions.query_db:2']

if isinstance(books_raw, str) and books_raw.endswith('.json'):
    # It's a file path
    with open(books_raw, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_raw

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Try to extract year from details
years = []
no_year_count = 0
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    if not details:
        no_year_count += 1
        continue
    # Look for 4-digit years
    year_matches = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_matches:
        # Usually the publication year is between 1900-2030
        for year in year_matches:
            year_int = int(year)
            if 1900 <= year_int <= 2030:
                years.append(year_int)
                break
    else:
        no_year_count += 1

print(f'Extracted years from {len(years)} books')
print(f'Could not find year for {no_year_count} books')
if years:
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample years: {years[:10]}')"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Access the books data
books_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:2']

# Load books data (it's stored in a file)
if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_storage

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Extract publication years from details field
year_book_mapping = []
no_year_count = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    if not details:
        no_year_count += 1
        continue
    
    # Look for patterns like "released on January 1, 2004" or "published on May 20, 1996"
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Check if it's a reasonable publication year
        if 1900 <= year <= 2030:
            year_book_mapping.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'title': book['title'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': f"{year//10*10}s"
            })
        else:
            no_year_count += 1
    else:
        no_year_count += 1

print(f'Successfully extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books')
print(f'Failed to extract year for {no_year_count} books')

if year_book_mapping:
    years = [item['year'] for item in year_book_mapping]
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample data: {year_book_mapping[:3]}')

# Now load the review data to get ratings
review_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:9']
if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    review_data = review_storage

print(f'\nLoaded {len(review_data)} reviews')
print(f'Sample review: {review_data[0]}')

# Create mapping of purchase_id to rating
purchase_rating_map = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    if purchase_id:
        if purchase_id not in purchase_rating_map:
            purchase_rating_map[purchase_id] = []
        purchase_rating_map[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f'Found {len(purchase_rating_map)} unique books with reviews')

# Match books with their reviews and calculate average rating per decade
from collections import defaultdict

decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'books': set()})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Look for purchase_id that matches book_id pattern
    # The hint says purchase_id and book_id refer to same entities but aren't exact matches
    # Let's try to match by extracting the numeric part
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        book_num = match.group(1)
        # Look for purchase_id with same number
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_rating_map.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate average
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'total_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade statistics (with at least 10 books):')
for result in decade_results:
    print(f"{result['decade']}: {result['avg_rating']} avg rating, {result['book_count']} books, {result['total_reviews']} reviews")

# Return the top decade
if decade_results:
    top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print(f'\nTop decade: {top_decade}')
else:
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')
    top_decade = None

# Print results in JSON format
output = {
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'decade_rankings': decade_results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Access the books data
books_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:2']

# Load books data (it's stored in a file)
if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_storage

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Extract publication years from details field
year_book_mapping = []
no_year_count = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    if not details:
        no_year_count += 1
        continue
    
    # Look for 4-digit year patterns
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Check if it's a reasonable publication year
        if 1900 <= year <= 2030:
            year_book_mapping.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'title': book['title'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': str(year//10*10) + 's'
            })
        else:
            no_year_count += 1
    else:
        no_year_count += 1

print(f'Successfully extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books')
print(f'Failed to extract year for {no_year_count} books')

if year_book_mapping:
    years = [item['year'] for item in year_book_mapping]
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')

# Now load the review data to get ratings
review_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:9']
if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    review_data = review_storage

print(f'\nLoaded {len(review_data)} reviews')

# Create mapping of purchase_id to rating
purchase_rating_map = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating', '0')
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        rating = 0.0
    
    if purchase_id:
        if purchase_id not in purchase_rating_map:
            purchase_rating_map[purchase_id] = []
        purchase_rating_map[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f'Found {len(purchase_rating_map)} unique books with reviews')

# Match books with their reviews and calculate average rating per decade
from collections import defaultdict

decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'books': set()})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Extract numeric part from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" -> "1")
    book_id_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if book_id_match:
        book_num = book_id_match.group(1)
        # Look for purchase_id containing the same number
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_rating_map.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
                break  # Found match, move to next book

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate average
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'total_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade statistics (with at least 10 books):')
for result in decade_results:
    print(str(result['decade']) + ': ' + str(result['avg_rating']) + ' avg rating, ' + 
          str(result['book_count']) + ' books, ' + str(result['total_reviews']) + ' reviews')

# Return the top decade
if decade_results:
    top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print('\nTop decade: ' + top_decade)
else:
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')
    top_decade = None

# Print results in JSON format
output = {
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'decade_rankings': decade_results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

    # Extract numeric part from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" -> "1")
    book_id_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if book_id_match:
        book_num = book_id_match.group(1)
        # Look for purchase_id containing the same number
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_rating_map.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
                break  # Found match, move to next book

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate average
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'total_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade statistics (with at least 10 books):')
for result in decade_results:
    result_str = str(result['decade']) + ': ' + str(result['avg_rating']) + ' avg rating, ' + str(result['book_count']) + ' books, ' + str(result['total_reviews']) + ' reviews'
    print(result_str)

# Return the top decade
if decade_results:
    top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print('\nTop decade: ' + top_decade)
else:
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')
    top_decade = None

# Print results in JSON format
output = {
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'decade_rankings': decade_results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

            year_book_mapping.append({

                'title': book['title'],
                'year': year,

for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():

        decade_results.append({

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Storage keys should be available
print("Available storage keys:")
for key in list(locals().keys()):
    if 'var_functions' in key:
        print(f"  {key}: {type(locals()[key])}")

# Load books data from storage
books_key = 'var_functions.query_db:24'
if books_key not in locals():
    print("Books data not found in storage")
    books_data = []
else:
    books_storage = locals()[books_key]
    if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
        with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
            books_data = json.load(f)
    else:
        books_data = books_storage
    print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books")

# Load review data from storage  
review_key = 'var_functions.query_db:9'
if review_key not in locals():
    print("Review data not found in storage")
    review_data = []
else:
    review_storage = locals()[review_key]
    if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
        with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
            review_data = json.load(f)
    else:
        review_data = review_storage
    print(f"Loaded {len(review_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication years from books
year_book_mapping = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit year between 1900-2030
    match = re.search(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-3]\d)', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        year_book_mapping.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books")

# Create purchase_id -> ratings mapping
purchase_ratings = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    if purchase_id:
        try:
            rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
            if purchase_id not in purchase_ratings:
                purchase_ratings[purchase_id] = []
            purchase_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)
        except:
            continue

print(f"Mapped ratings for {len(purchase_ratings)} purchase IDs")

# Calculate decade statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'books': set(), 'review_count': 0})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Extract numeric part from book_id
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        book_num = match.group(1)
        # Find matching purchase_id
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_ratings.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:  # Fuzzy match by number
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                break

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count
        })

# Sort by rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"\nFound {len(decade_results)} decades with >=10 books")
print("Rankings:")
for r in decade_results:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}: {r['avg_rating']} ({r['book_count']} books)")

# Get top decade
top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else None

output = {'top_decade': top_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

2. I queried the review_database to get review data with purchase_id and rating
3. I extracted publication years from the "details" field using regex patterns for 4-digit years (1900-2030)
4. I extracted the numeric part from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" -> "1")
5. I matched books to reviews by finding purchase_id containing the same numeric part
6. For each matched book, I calculated its average rating across all reviews
7. I grouped books by decade (e.g., 2010s, 2000s, etc.)
8. I calculated the average rating per decade by taking the mean of each book's average rating
9. I filtered to only include decades with at least 10 distinct books
10. I ranked the decades by average rating

The result shows only one decade met the criteria: the 2010s with 10 books and an average rating of 4.0. This makes it the top decade by default as it's the only one with at least 10 books.

So the answer is "2010s".

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. {"code": "import json
import re

# Access the books data
books_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:2']

# Load books data (it's stored in a file)
if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_storage

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Extract publication years from details field
year_book_mapping = []
no_year_count = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    if not details:
        no_year_count += 1
        continue
    
    # Look for patterns like "released on January 1, 2004" or "published on May 20, 1996"
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Check if it's a reasonable publication year
        if 1900 <= year <= 2030:
            year_book_mapping.append({
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'title': book['title'],
                'year': year,
                'decade': f"{year//10*10}s"
            })
        else:
            no_year_count += 1
    else:
        no_year_count += 1

print(f'Successfully extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books')
print(f'Failed to extract year for {no_year_count} books')

if year_book_mapping:
    years = [item['year'] for item in year_book_mapping]
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample data: {year_book_mapping[:3]}')

# Now load the review data to get ratings
review_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:9']
if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    review_data = review_storage

print(f'\nLoaded {len(review_data)} reviews')
print(f'Sample review: {review_data[0]}')

# Create mapping of purchase_id to rating
purchase_rating_map = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
    if purchase_id:
        if purchase_id not in purchase_rating_map:
            purchase_rating_map[purchase_id] = []
        purchase_rating_map[purchase_id].append(rating)

print(f'Found {len(purchase_rating_map)} unique books with reviews')

# Match books with their reviews and calculate average rating per decade
from collections import defaultdict

decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'books': set()})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Look for purchase_id that matches book_id pattern
    # The hint says purchase_id and book_id refer to same entities but aren't exact matches
    # Let's try to match by extracting the numeric part
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        book_num = match.group(1)
        # Look for purchase_id with same number
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_rating_map.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate average
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'total_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('\nDecade statistics (with at least 10 books):')
for result in decade_results:
    print(f"{result['decade']}: {result['avg_rating']} avg rating, {result['book_count']} books, {result['total_reviews']} reviews")

# Return the top decade
if decade_results:
    top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade']
    print(f'\nTop decade: {top_decade}')
else:
    print('\nNo decades found with at least 10 books')
    top_decade = None

# Print results in JSON format
output = {
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'decade_rankings': decade_results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Storage keys should be available
print("Available storage keys:")
for key in list(locals().keys()):
    if 'var_functions' in key:
        print(f"  {key}: {type(locals()[key])}")

# Load books data from storage
books_key = 'var_functions.query_db:24'
if books_key not in locals():
    print("Books data not found in storage")
    books_data = []
else:
    books_storage = locals()[books_key]
    if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
        with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
            books_data = json.load(f)
    else:
        books_data = books_storage
    print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books")

# Load review data from storage  
review_key = 'var_functions.query_db:9'
if review_key not in locals():
    print("Review data not found in storage")
    review_data = []
else:
    review_storage = locals()[review_key]
    if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
        with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
            review_data = json.load(f)
    else:
        review_data = review_storage
    print(f"Loaded {len(review_data)} reviews")

# Extract publication years from books
year_book_mapping = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find 4-digit year between 1900-2030
    match = re.search(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-3]\d)', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        year_book_mapping.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Extracted years for {len(year_book_mapping)} books")

# Create purchase_id -> ratings mapping
purchase_ratings = {}
for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    if purchase_id:
        try:
            rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
            if purchase_id not in purchase_ratings:
                purchase_ratings[purchase_id] = []
            purchase_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)
        except:
            continue

print(f"Mapped ratings for {len(purchase_ratings)} purchase IDs")

# Calculate decade statistics
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_rating': 0, 'books': set(), 'review_count': 0})

for book in year_book_mapping:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    # Extract numeric part from book_id
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        book_num = match.group(1)
        # Find matching purchase_id
        for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_ratings.items():
            if book_num in purchase_id:  # Fuzzy match by number
                avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
                decade = book['decade']
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += avg_rating
                decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += len(ratings)
                break

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and calculate averages
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    book_count = len(stats['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / book_count
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'book_count': book_count
        })

# Sort by rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"\nFound {len(decade_results)} decades with >=10 books")
print("Rankings:")
for r in decade_results:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}: {r['avg_rating']} ({r['book_count']} books)")

# Get top decade
top_decade = decade_results[0]['decade'] if decade_results else None

output = {'top_decade': top_decade}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load books data
books_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
books_data = []
if isinstance(books_storage, str) and books_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(books_storage, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_storage

# Load review data
review_storage = locals()['var_functions.query_db:9']
review_data = []
if isinstance(review_storage, str) and review_storage.endswith('.json'):
    with open(review_storage, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    review_data = review_storage

print('Books:', len(books_data))
print('Reviews:', len(review_data))

# Extract years and create book-year mapping
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    m = re.search(r'(19\d{2}|20[0-3]\d)', details)
    if m:
        year = int(m.group(1))
        book_years[book['book_id']] = year

print('Books with year:', len(book_years))

# Map purchase_id to ratings
purchase_ratings = {}
for review in review_data:
    pid = review.get('purchase_id')
    if pid:
        try:
            rating = float(review.get('rating', 0))
            purchase_ratings.setdefault(pid, []).append(rating)
        except:
            pass

print('Purchase IDs:', len(purchase_ratings))

# Calculate decade stats
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'total': 0, 'books': set()})

for book_id, year in book_years.items():
    book_num = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if not book_num: continue
    book_num = book_num.group(1)
    
    for purchase_id, ratings in purchase_ratings.items():
        if book_num in purchase_id:
            avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
            decade = str((year // 10) * 10) + 's'
            decades[decade]['total'] += avg
            decades[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
            break

# Filter and rank
results = []
for decade, data in decades.items():
    book_count = len(data['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = data['total'] / book_count
        results.append([decade, round(avg_rating, 2), book_count])

results.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print('Decades with >=10 books:', len(results))
for r in results:
    print(r[0], r[1], r[2])

top = results[0][0] if results else None
print('Top decade:', top)

out = {'top_decade': top, 'all': results}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(out))"}

3. We need to match books (book_id) in books_database with reviews (purchase_id) in review_database
4. Calculate average rating per book
5. Group by decade and calculate average rating per decade
6. Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
7. Return the decade with the highest average rating

From the query results, I can see:
- The review database has books with purchase_ids (e.g., "purchaseid_186", "purchaseid_191", etc.)
- The book database has books with book_ids (e.g., "bookid_1", "bookid_2", etc.)
- The hint says we can join using a fuzzy join approach since they refer to the same entities

Let me use regex to extract years from the details field and match books with reviews.

I'll need to:
1. Query books_info to get book_id and details
2. Query review to get purchase_id and rating
3. Use Python to:
   - Extract years from details text using regex
   - Map book numbers to decades
   - Match book_id numbers (e.g., "1" from "bookid_1") with purchase_id numbers (e.g., "186" from "purchaseid_186")
   - Calculate average rating per book
   - Group by decade and calculate average
   - Filter decades with >= 10 books
   - Find the decade with highest average rating

Let me execute the queries and process the data.

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:9 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books_info data
with open(var_functions.query_db:2, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Check the details field to understand the structure
print('Sample details fields:')
for i, book in enumerate(books_data[:5]):
    print(f"Book {i+1}: {book['details'][:200]}")
    
# Try to extract year from details
years = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Look for 4-digit years
    year_matches = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_matches:
        # Usually the publication year is between 1900-2030
        for year in year_matches:
            year_int = int(year)
            if 1900 <= year_int <= 2030:
                years.append(year_int)
                break

print(f'\nExtracted {len(years)} years from {len(books_data)} books')
if years:
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample years: {years[:10]}')"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books_info data from the storage variable
books_raw = locals()['var_functions.query_db:2']

if isinstance(books_raw, str) and books_raw.endswith('.json'):
    # It's a file path
    with open(books_raw, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
else:
    books_data = books_raw

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Try to extract year from details
years = []
no_year_count = 0
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    if not details:
        no_year_count += 1
        continue
    # Look for 4-digit years
    year_matches = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_matches:
        # Usually the publication year is between 1900-2030
        for year in year_matches:
            year_int = int(year)
            if 1900 <= year_int <= 2030:
                years.append(year_int)
                break
    else:
        no_year_count += 1

print(f'Extracted years from {len(years)} books')
print(f'Could not find year for {no_year_count} books')
if years:
    print(f'Year range: {min(years)} - {max(years)}')
    print(f'Sample years: {years[:10]}')"}
